title: 第七講
en: OUR analysis of some of the nominal and verbal formations in the Aryan or Indo-European family of speech has taught us that, however mysterious and complicated these grammatical forms appear at first sight, they are in reality the result of a very simple process. It seems at first almost hopeless to ask such questions as why the addition of a mere d should change love present into love past, or why the termination ai in French, if added to aimer, should convey the idea of love to come. But, once placed under the microscope of comparative grammar, these and all other grammatical forms assume a very different and much more intelligible aspect. We saw how what we now call terminations were originally independent words. After coalescing with the words which they were intended to modify, they were gradually reduced to mere syllables and letters, unmeaning in themselves, yet manifesting their former power and independence by the modification which they continue to produce in the meaning of the words to which they are appended. The true nature of grammatical terminations was first pointed out by a philosopher, who, however wild some of his speculations may be, had certainly caught many a glimpse of the real life and growth of language ; I TERMINATIONS. 261 mean Home Tooke. This is what he writes of terminations :* —

4 For though I think I have good reasons to believe that all terminations may likewise be traced to their respective origin ; and that, however artificial they may now appear to us, they were not originally the effect of premeditated and deliberate art, but separate words by length of time corrupted and coalescing with the words of which they are now considered as the terminations; yet this was less likely to be suspected by others. And if it had been suspected, they would have had much further to travel to their journey's end, and through a road much more embarrassed ; as the corruption in those languages is of much longer standing than in ours, and more complex.'

Home Tooke, however, though he saw rightly what road should be followed to track the origin of grammatical terminations, was himself without the means to reach his journey's end. Most of his explanations are quite untenable, and it is curious to observe in reading his book, the Diversions of Purley, how a man of a clear, sharp, and powerful mind, and reasoning according to sound and correct principles, may yet, owing to his defective knowledge of facts, arrive at conclusions directly opposed to truth.

When we have once seen how grammatical terminations are to be traced back in the beginning to independent words, we have learnt at the same time that the component elements of language, which remain in our crucible at the end of a complete 262 ROOTS.
grammatical analysis, are of two kinds, namely, Roots predicative and Roots demonstrative.

We call root or radical whatever, in the words of any language or family of languages, cannot be reduced to a simpler or more original form. It may be well to illustrate this by a few examples. But, instead of taking a number of words in Sanskrit, Greek, and Latin, and tracing them back to their common centre, it will be more instructive if we begin with a root which has been discovered, and follow it through its wanderings from language to language. I take the root AR, to which I alluded in our last Lecture as the source of the word Arya, and we shall thus, while examining its ramification, learn at the same time why that name was chosen by the agricultural nomads, the ancestors of the Aryan race.

This root AR* means to plough, to open the soil. From it we have the Latin ar-are, the Greek ar-oun, the Irish ar, the Lithuanian ar-ti, the Russian ora-ti, the Gothic ar-jan, the Anglo-Saxon er-jan, the modern English to ear. Shakespeare says (Richard II. in. 2), ' to ear the land that has some hope to grow.' We read in Deut. xxi. 4, ' a rough valley which is neither eared nor sown.'

From this we have the name of the plough, or the instrument of earing : in Latin, ara-trum ; in Greek, aro-tron ; in Bohemian, oradlo ; in Lithuanian, arkla-s ; in Cornish, aradar ; in Welsh, arad ; j* in (Pott, Etymologische Forschungen, i. 218); but for our present purposes the root AR. is sufficient.
•\ If, as has been supposed, the Cornish and Welsh words were corruptions of the Latin aratrum, they would have appeared as areuder, arawd, respectively.

HOOT AR. 263 Old Norse, ardhr. In Old Norse, however, ardhr, meaning originally the plough, came to mean earnings or wealth ; the plough being, in early times, the most essential possession and means of livelihood. In the same manner the Latin name for money, pecunia, was derived from pecus, cattle; the word fee, which is now restricted to the payment made to a doctor or lawyer, was in Old English feh, and in Anglo-Saxon feoh, meaning cattle and wealth ; for feoh, and Gothic faihu, are really the same word as the Latin pecus, the modern German vieh.

The act of ploughing is called aratio in Latin ; arosis in Greek : and I believe that aroma, too, in the sense of perfume, had the same origin ; for what is sweeter or more aromatic than the smell of a ploughed field? In Genesis xxviii. 27, Jacob says 'the smell of my son is as the smell of a field which the Lord has blessed.'

A more primitive formation of the root ar seems to be the Greek era, earth, the Sanskrit ira, the Old High-German ero, the Gaelic ire, irionn. It meant originally the ploughed land, afterwards earth in general. Even the word earth, the Gothic airtha* the Anglo-Saxon eorthe, must have been taken originally in the sense of ploughed or cultivated land. The derivative ar-mentum, formed like ju-mentum, would arjan, on account of the difference in the vowels. But airtha is a much more ancient formation, and comes from the root ar, which root, again, was originally ri or ir (Benfey, Kurze Gr. p. 27). From this primitive root ri or ir, we must derive both the Sanskrit ira or ida, and the Gothic airtha. The latter would correspond to the Sanskrit rita. The true meaning of the Sanskrit ida has never been discovered. The Brahmans explain it as prayer, but this is not its original meaning.
naturally have been applied to any animal fit for ploughing and other labour in the field, whether ox or horse.

As agriculture was the principal labour in that early state of society when we must suppose most of our Aryan words to have been formed and applied to their definite meanings, we may well understand how a word which originally meant this special kind of labour was afterwards used to signify labour in general. The most natural tendency in the growth of words and of their meanings is from the special to the general : thus regere and gubernare, which originally meant to steer a ship, took the general sense of governing. To equip, which originally was to furnish a ship (French equiper and esquif, from schifo, ship), came to mean furnishing in general. Now in modern German, arbeit means simply labour ; arbeitsam means industrious. In Gothic, too, arbaips is only used to express labour and trouble in general. But in Old Norse, erfidhi means chiefly ploughing, and afterwards labour in general; and the same word in Anglo-Saxon, earfodh or earfedhe, is labour. Of course we might equally suppose that, as labourer, from meaning one who labours in general, came to take the special sense of an agricultural labourer, so arbeit, from meaning work in general, came to be applied, in Old Norse, to the work of ploughing. But as the root of erfidhi seems to be ar, our first explanation is the more plausible. Besides, the simple ar in Old Norse means ploughing and labour, and the Old High-German art has likewise the sense of ploughing.* arapeit, Modern High-German arbeit, directly from the Gothic arbja, heir; but admits a relationship between arbja and the KOOT AR. 265 The Greek Aroura and the Latin arvum, a field, have to be referred to the root ar, to plough. And as ploughing was not only one of the earliest kinds of labour, but also one of the most primitive arts, I have no doubt that the Latin ars, artis, and our own word art, meant originally the art of all arts, first taught to mortals by the goddess of all wisdom, the art of cultivating the land. In Old High-German arunti, in Anglo-Saxon cerend, means simply work ; but they too must originally have meant the special work of agriculture ; and in the English errand, and errand-boy, the same word is still in existence.
But ar did not only mean to plough, or to cut open the land; it was transferred at a very early time to the ploughing of the sea, or rowing. Thus Shakspeare says : —
Make the sea serve them ; which they ear and wound With keels.

In a similar manner, we find that Sanskrit derives from ar the substantive aritra, not in the sense of a plough, but in the sense of a rudder. In AngloSaxon we find the simple form ar, the English oar, as it were the plough-share of the water. The Greek also had used the root ar in the sense of rowing ; for eretes* in Greek is a rower, and their word tri-er-es, root arjan, to plough. He identifies arbja with the Slavonic rab, servant, slave, and arbeit with rabota, corvee, supposing that sons and heirs were the first natural slaves. He supposes even a relationship between rabota and the Latin labor (German Dictionary, s. v. Arbeit).
fioe. From Ipe'rTje, epeVffw ; and VTTTJPET-J/C) servant, helper. Rostrum from rodere.
meant originally a ship with three oars, or with three rows of oars,* a trireme.

This comparison of ploughing and rowing is of frequent occurrence in ancient languages. The English word plough, the Slavonic ploug, has been identified with the Sanskrit plava^ a ship, and with the Greek ploion, ship. As the Aryans spoke of a ship ploughing the sea, they also spoke of a plough sailing across the field ; and thus it was that the same names were applied to both. J In English dialects, plough or plow is still used in the general sense of wagon or conveyance. § We might follow the offshoots of this root ar still further, but the number of words which we have examined in various languages will suffice to show what is meant by a predicative root. In all these words ar is the radical element, all the rest is merely formative. The root ar is called a predicative root, because in whatever composition it enters, it predicates one and the same conception, whether of the plough, or the rudder, or the ox, or the field. Even oars on both sides.
f From Sanskrit plu, TrXe'w : cf. fleet and float.

ROOT SPAC. 267 in such a word as artistic, the predicative power of the root ar may still be perceived, though, of course, as it were by means of a powerful telescope only. The Brahmans, who call themselves drya in India, were no more aware of the real origin of this name and its connection with agricultural labour, than the artist who now speaks of his art as a divine inspiration suspects that the word which he uses was originally applicable only to so primitive an art as that of ploughing.
We shall now examine another family of words, in order to see by what process the radical elements of words were first discovered.

Let us take the word respectable* It is a word of Latin, not of Saxon origin. In respectabilis we easily distinguish the verb respectare and the termination bilis. We then separate the prefix re, which leaves spectare, and we trace spectare as a participial formation back to the Latin verb spicere or specere, meaning to see, to look. In specere, again, we distinguish between the changeable termination ere and the unchangeable remnant spec, which we call the root. This root we expect to find in Sanskrit and the other Aryan languages ; and so we do. In Sanskrit the more usual form is pa's, to see, without the s ; but spa's also is found in spa'sa, a spy, in spashta and vi-spashta, clear, manifest, and in the Vedic spas, a guardian. In the Teutonic family we find spehdn in Old High-German meaning to look, to spy, to contemplate ; and speha, the English spy.* In Greek, the root spek has been changed into sleep, which exists isches Wurzelworterbuch, p. 236.

in skeptomai, I look, I examine ; from whence skeptikos, an examiner or inquirer, in theological language, a sceptic ; and episkopos, an overseer, a bishop. Let us now examine the various ramifications of this root. Beginning with respectable, we found that it originally meant a person who deserves Respect, respect meaning looking back. We pass by common objects or persons without noticing them, whereas we turn back to look again at those which deserve our admiration, our regard, our respect. This was the original meaning of respect and respectable, nor need we be surprised at this if we consider that noble, nobilis in Latin, conveyed originally no more than the idea of a person that deserves to be known ; for nobilis stands for gnobilis, just as nomen stands for gnomen, or natus for gnatus.
4 With respect to ' has now become almost a mere preposition. For if we say, ' With respect to this point I have no more to say,' this is the same as ' I have no more to say on this point.'
Again, as in looking back we single out a person, the adjective respective, and the adverb respectively, are used almost in the same sense as special, or singly.

The English respite is the Norman modification of respectus, the French repit. Repit meant originally looking back, reviewing the whole evidence. A criminal received so many days ad respectum, to re-examinj| the case. Afterwards it was said that the prisoner had received a respit, that is to say, had obtained a re-examination ; and at last a verb was formed, and it was said that a person had been respited.

As specere, to see, with the preposition re, came BOOT SPAC. 269 to mean respect, so with the preposition de, down, it forms the Latin despicere, meaning to look down, the English despise. The French depit (Old French despii) means no longer contempt, though it is the Latin despectus, but rather anger, vexation. Se depiter is, to be vexed, to fret. *• En depit de luij is originally ' angry with him,' then ' in spite of him ; ' and the English spite, in spite of, spiteful, are mere abbreviations of despite, in despite of, despiteful, and having nothing whatever to do with the spitting of cats.

As de means down from above, so sub means up from below, and this added to specere, to look, gives us suspicere, suspicari, to look up, in the sense of to suspect.* From it suspicion, suspicious ; and likewise the French soupqon, even in such phrases as ' There is a soupgon of chicory in this coffee,' meaning just a touch, just the smallest atom of chicory.
As circum means round about, so circumspect means, of course, cautious, careful.
With in, meaning into, specere forms inspicere, to inspect ; hence inspector, inspection.
With ad towards, specere becomes adspicere, to look at a thing. Hence adspectus, the aspect, the look or appearance of things.

So with pro, forward, specere became prospicere ; and gave rise to such words as prospectus, as it were a look out, prospective, &c. With con, with, spicere forms conspicere, to see together, conspectus, con- which is connected with SlpKop.™, I see; the Sanskrit dris. In Sanskrit, however, the more primitive root dri, or dar, has likewise been preserved, and is of frequent occurrence, particularly if joined with the preposition a ; tad adritya, with respect to this.

spicuous. We saw before in respectable, that a new word, spectare, is formed from the participle of spicere. This, with the preposition ex, out, gives us the Latin expectare, the English to expect, to look out ; with its derivatives.

Auspicious is another word which contains our root as the second of its component elements. The Latin auspicium stands for avispicium,, and meant the looking out for certain birds which were considered to be of good or bad omen to the success of any public or private act. Hence auspicious is the sense of lucky. Haru-spex was the name given to a person who foretold the future from the inspection of the entrails of animals.
Again, from specere, speculum was formed, in the sense of looking-glass, or any other means of looking at oneself ; and from it speculari, the English to speculate, speculative, &c.

But there are many more offshoots of this one root. Thus, the Latin speculum, looking-glass, became specchio in Italian ; and the same word, though in a roundabout way, came into French, as the adjective espiegle, waggish. The origin of this French word is curious. There exists in German a famous cycle of stories, mostly tricks played by a halfhistorical, half-mythical character of the name of Eulenspiegel, or Owl-glass. These stories were translated into French, and the hero was known at first by the name of Ulespiegle, which name, contracted afterwards into Espiegle, became a general name for every wag.

As the French borrowed not only from Latin, but likewise from the Teutonic languages, we meet there, side by side with the derivatives of the Latin specere, BOOT SPAC. 271 the Old High- German spe'Jwn, slightly disguised as epier, to spy, the Italian spiare. The German word for a spy was speha, and this appears in old French as espie, in modern French as espion.

One of the most prolific branches of the same root is the Latin species. Whether we take species in the sense of a perennial succession of similar individuals in continual generations (Jussieu), or look upon it as existing only as a category of thought (Agassiz), species was intended originally as the literal translation of the Greek eidos as opposed to genos, or genus. The Greeks classified things originally according to kind and form, and though these terms were afterwards technically defined by Aristotle, their etymological meaning is in reality the most appropriate. Things may be classified either because they are of the same genus or kind, that is to say, because they had the same origin ; this gives us a genealogical classification : or they can be classified because they have the same appearance, eidos, or form, without claiming for them a common origin ; and this gives us a morphological classification. It was, however, in the Aristotelian, and not in its etymological sense, that the Greek eidos was rendered in Latin by species, meaning the subdivision of a genus, the class of a family. Hence the French espece, a kind ; the English special, in the sense of particular as opposed to general. There is little of the root spas, to see, left in a special train, or a special messenger', yet the connection, though not apparent, can be restored with perfect certainty. We frequently hear the expression to specify. A man specifies his grievances. What does it mean ? The mediaeval Latin specificus is a literal translation of the Greek 272 CLASSES OF ROOTS.

eidopoios. This means what makes or constitutes an eidos or species. Now, in classification, what constitutes a species is that particular quality which, superadded to other qualities, shared in common by all the members of a genus, distinguishes one class from all other classes. Thus the specific character which distinguishes man from all other animals is reason or language. Specific, therefore, assumed the sense of distinguishing or distinct, and the verb to specify conveyed the meaning of enumerating distinctly, or one by one. I finish with the French epicier, a respectable grocer, but originally a man who sold drugs. The different kinds of drugs which the apothecary had to sell were spoken of, with a certain learned air, as species, not as drugs in general, but as peculiar drugs and special medicines. Hence the chymist or apothecary is still called speziale in Italian, his shop spezieria* In French species, which regularly became espece, assumed a new form to express drugs, namely, epices ; the English spices, the German Spezereien. Hence the famous pain d1 epices, gingerbread nuts, and epicier, a grocer. If you try for a moment to trace spicy, or a well-spiced article, back to the simple root specere, to look, you will understand that marvellous power of language which, out of a few simple elements, has created a variety of names hardly surpassed by the unbounded variety of nature herself, f I say 'out of a few simple elements/ for the generos, merchandise.
f Many derivatives might have been added, such as specimen, spectator, le spectacle, specialite, spectrum, spectacles, specious, specula, &c.
CLASSES OF BOOTS. 273 number of what we call full predicative roots, such as ar, to plough, or spas, to look, is indeed small.

A root is necessarily monosyllabic.* Roots consisting of more than one syllable can always be proved to be derivative foots, and even among monosyllabic roots it is necessary to distinguish between primitive, secondary, and tertiary roots.
A. Primitive roots are those which consist (1) of one vowel ; for instance, i, to go.
(2) of one vowel and one consonant ; for instance, ad, to eat.
(3 ) of one consonant and one vowel ; for instance, da, to give.
B. Secondary roots are those which consist ( 1 ) of one consonant, vowel, and consonant ; for instance, tud, to strike.
In these roots either the first or the last consonant is modificatory.
C. Tertiary roots are those which consist ( 1 ) of consonant, consonant, and vowel ; for in- stance, plu, to flow.
(2) of vowel, consonant, and consonant; for in- stance, ard, to hurt.
(3) of consonant, consonant, vowel, and conso- nant ; for instance, spa's, to see.
(4) of consonant, consonant, vowel, consonant, and consonant; for instance, spand, to tremble.
The primary roots are the most important in the Forsch.il pp. 216, 311.

early history of language ; but their predicative power being generally of too indefinite a character to answer the purposes of advancing thought, they were soon encroached upon and almost supplanted by secondary and tertiary radicals.

In the secondary roots we can frequently observe "that one of the consonants, in the Aryan languages generally the final, is liable to modification. The root retains its general meaning, which is slightly modified and determined by the changes of the final consonants. Thus, besides tud (tudati), we have in Sanskrit tup (topati, tupati, and tumpati), meaning to strike ; Greek typ-to. We meet likewise with tubh (tubhndti, tubhyati, tobhate), to strike ; and, according to Sanskrit grammarians, with tuph (tophati, tuphati, tumphati). Then there is a root tuj (tunjati, tojati), to strike, to excite ; another root, tur (tutorti), to which the same meaning is ascribed ; another, tur (turyate), to hurt. Then there is the further derivative turv (turvati), to strike, to conquer ; there is tuh (tohati\ to pain, to vex ; and there is tus (to'tate), to which Sanskrit grfl.TnTnfl.ria.Tis attribute the sense of striking.

Although we may call all these verbal bases roots, they stand to the first class in about the same relation as the triliteral Semitic roots to the more primitive biliteral.* In the third class we shall find that one of the two consonants is always a semivowel, nasal, or sibilant, these being more variable than the other consonants ; and we can almost always point to one consonant as of later origin, and added to a biconsonantal root in CLASSES OF ROOTS. 275 order to render its meaning more special. Thus we have, besides spas, the root pa's, and even this root has been traced back by Pott to a more primitive as. Thus vand, again, is a mere strengthening of the root vad, like mand of mad, like yu-na-j and yu-n-j of yuj. The root yuj, to join, and yudh, to fight, both point back to a root yu, to mingle, and this simple root has been preserved in Sanskrit. We may well understand that a root, having the general meaning of mingling or being together, should be employed to express both the friendly joining of hands and the engaging in hostile combat ; but we may equally understand that language, in its progress to clearness and definiteness, should have desired a distinction between these two meanings, and should gladly have availed herself of the two derivatives, yuj and yudh, to mark this distinction.

Sanskrit grammarians have reduced the whole growth of their language to 1,706 roots,* that is to say, they have admitted so many radicals in order to derive from them, according to their system of grammatical derivation, all nouns, verbs, adjectives, pronouns, prepositions, adverbs, and conjunctions, which occur in Sanskrit. According to our explanation of a root, however, this number of 1,706 would have to be reduced considerably, and though a few new roots would likewise have to be added which Sanskrit grammarians failed to discover, yet the number of Boots of the 2, 3, 5, 7, 8, 9 classes . . 226 Roots of the 1, 4, 6, 10 classes . . . 1,480 including 143 of the 10th class. T 2 276 NUMBER OF ROOTS AND WORDS.

primitive sounds, expressive of definite meanings, requisite for the etymological analysis of the whole Sanskrit dictionary would not amount to even onethird of that number. Hebrew has been reduced to about 500 roots,* and I doubt whether we want a larger number for Sanskrit. This shows a wise spirit of economy on the part of primitive language, for the possibility of forming new roots for every new impression was almost unlimited. Even if we put the number of letters only at twenty-four, the possible number of biliteral and triliteral roots would amount together to 14,400 ;f whereas Chinese, though abstaining from composition and derivation, and therefore requiring a larger number of radicals than any other language, was satisfied with about 450. With these 450 sounds, raised to 1,263 by various accents and intonations, the Chinese have produced a dictionary of from 40,000 to 50,000 words.J counted 5,642 Hebrew and Chaldee words in the O. T. ' 277 It is clear, however, that in addition to these predicative roots, we want another class of radical elements to enable us to account for the full growth of language. With the 400 or 500 predicative roots at her disposal, language would not have been at a loss to coin names for all things that come under our cognisance. Language is a thrifty housewife. Consider the variety of ideas that were expressed by the one root spas, and you will see that with 500 such roots she might form a dictionary sufficient to satisfy the wants, however extravagant, of her husband — the human mind. If each root yielded fifty derivatives, we should have 25,000 words. Now, we are told on good authority, by a country clergyman, that some of the labourers in his parish had not 300 words in their vocabulary.* The cuneiform inscriptions of Persia contain no more than 379 words, 131 of these being proper names. The vocabulary of the ancient sages of Egypt, at least as far as it is known to us from the hieroglyphic inscriptions, amounts to about 658 words.f The libretto of an Italian opera number of the classical characters is 42,718. Many of them are no longer in use in the modern language, but they occur in the canonical and the classical books. They may be found sometimes in official documents, when an attempt is made at imitating the old style. A considerable portion of these are names of persons, places, mountains, rivers, &c. In order to compete for the place of imperial historian, it was necessary to know 9,000, which were collected in a separate manual.' — Stanislas Julien.

seldom displays a greater variety.* A well-educated person in England, who has been at a public school and at the university, who reads his Bible, his Shakspeare, the Times, and all the books of Mudie's Library, seldom uses more than about 3,000 or 4,000 words in actual conversation. Accurate thinkers and close reasoners, who avoid vague and general expressions, and wait till they find the word that exactly fits their meaning, employ a larger stock ; and eloquent speakers mayrise to a command of 10,000. Shakspeare, who displayed a greater variety of expression than probably any writer in any language, produced all his plays with about 15,000 words. DEMONSTRATIVE BOOTS. 279 Five hundred roots, therefore, considering their fertility and pliancy, was more than was wanted for the dictionary of our primitive ancestors. And yet they wanted something more. If they had a root expressive of light and splendour, that root might have formed the predicate in the names of sun, and moon, and stars, and heaven, day, morning, dawn, spring, gladness, joy, beauty, majesty, love, friend, gold, riches, &c. But if they wanted to express here and there, who, what, this, that, thou, he, they would have found it impossible to discover any predicative root that could be applied to this purpose. Attempts have indeed been made to trace these words back to predicative roots ; but if we are told that the demonstrative root ta, this or there, may be derived from a predicative root tan, to extend, we find that even in our modern languages, the demonstrative pronouns and particles are of too primitive and independent a nature to allow of so artificial an interpretation. The sound ta or sa, for this or there, is as involuntary, as natural, as independent an expression as any of the predicative roots, and although some of these demonstrative, or pronominal, or local roots, for all these names have been applied to them, may be traced back to a predicative source, we must admit a small class of independent radicals, not predicative in the usual sense of the word, but simply pointing, simply expressive of existence under certain more or less definite, local or temporal prescriptions.
It will be best to give one illustration at least of a pronominal root and its influence in the formation of words.
In some languages, and particularly in Chinese, a predicative root may by itself be used as a noun, or 280 DEMONSTRATIVE ROOTS.

a verb, or an adjective or adverb. Thus the Chinese sound ta means, without any change of form, great, greatness, and to be great.* If ta stands before a substantive, it has the meaning of an adjective. Thus ta jin means a great man. If ta stands after a substantive, it is a predicate, or, as we should say, a verb. Thus, jin ta (or jin ta ye) would mean the man is great. f Or again, jin ngo, li pu ngo, would mean man bad, law not bad. Here we see that there is no outward distinction whatever between a root and a word, and that a noun is distinguished from a verb merely by its collocation in a sentence.

In other languages, however, and particularly in the Aryan languages, no predicative root can by itself form a word. Thus in Latin there is a root luc, to shine. In order to have a substantive, such as light, it was necessary to add a pronominal or demonstrative root, this forming the general subject of which the meaning contained in the root is to be predicated. Thus by the addition of the pronominal element s we have the Latin noun, luc-s, the light, or literally, shining-there. Let us add a personal pronoun, and we have the verb luc-e-s, shining-thou, thou shinest. Let us add other pronominal derivatives, and we get the adjectives, lucidus, luculentus, lucerna, &c.

It would be a totally mistaken view, however, were f If two words are placed like jin ta, the first may form the predicate of the second, the second being used as a substantive. Tims jin ta might mean the greatness of man, but in this case it is more usual to s&yjin tci ta.

' Another instance — chen, virtue ; ex. jin tchi chen, the virtue of man : chen, virtuous ; ex. chen jin, the virtuous man : chen, to approve ; ex. chen tchi, to find it good : chen, well ; ex. chen ko, to sing well.' — Stanislas Julien.

DEMONSTEATIVE ROOTS. '281 we to suppose that all derivative elements, all that remains of a word after the predicative root has been removed, must be traced back to pronominal roots. We have only to look at some of our own modern derivatives in order to be convinced that many of them were originally predicative, that they entered into composition with the principal predicative root, and then dwindled down to mere suffixes. Thus scape in landscape, and the more modern ship in hardship, are both derived from the same root which we have in Gothic,* skapa, skop, skopum, to create ; in Anglo-Saxon, scape, scdp, scopon. It is the same as the German derivative schaft, in Gesellschaft &c. So again dom in wisdom or Christendom is derived from the same root which we have in to do. It is the same as the German thum in Christenthum, the AngloSaxon dom in cyning-dom, Konigthum.^ Sometimes it may seem doubtful whether a derivative element was originally merely demonstrative or predicative. Thus the termination of the comparative in Sanskrit is tara, the Greek teros. This might, at first sight, be taken for a demonstrative element, but it is in reality the root tar, which means to go beyond, which we have likewise in the Latin trans. This trans in its French form tres is prefixed to adjectives in order to express a higher or transcendent degree, and the same root was well adapted to form the comparative in the ancient Aryan tongues. This root must like- f Spenser, Shepheard's Calender, Februarie 85 (ed. Collier, i. p. 25):—
' Cuddie, I wote thou kenst little good So. vainly t'advaunce thy headlesse hood :'
(for thy headlessness; hood is a termination denoting estate, as manhood. — T. Warton).

wise be admitted in one of the terminations of the locative which is tra in Sanskrit ; for instance, from ta, a demonstrative root, we form ta-tra, there, originally this way; we form anyatra, in another way; the same as in Latin we say ali-ter, irom aliud; compounds no more surprising than the French autrement (see p. 47) and the English otherwise.

Most of the terminations of declension and conjugation are demonstrative roots, and the s, for instance, of the third person singular, he loves, can be proved to have been originally the demonstrative pronoun of the third person. It was originally not s but t. This will require some explanation. The termination of the third person singular of the present is ti in Sanskrit. Thus da, to give, becomes dadati, he gives ; dhd, to place; dadhdti, he places.

In Greek this ti is changed into si; just as the Sanskrit tvam, the Lathi tu, thou, appears in Greek as sy. Thus Greek didosi corresponds to Sanskrit daddti; tithesi to dadhdti. In the course of time, however, every Greek s between two vowels, in a termination, was elided. Thus genos does not form the genitive genesos, like the Latin genus, genesis or generis, but geneos = genous. The dative is not genesi (the Latin generi), but gene'i = genei. In the same manner .all the regular verbs have ei for the termination of the third person singular. But this ei stands for esi. Thus typtei stands for typtesi, and this for typteti.
The Latin drops the final i, and instead of ti has t. Thus we get amat, dicit.

Now there is a law to which I alluded before, which is called Grimm's Law. According to it every tenuis in Latin is in Gothic represented by its corresponding aspirate. Hence, instead of t, we should ROOTS. 283 expect in Gothic th ; and so we find indeed in Gothic habaty, instead of Latin Tiabet. This aspirate likewise appears in Anglo-Saxon, where he loves is lufath. It is preserved in the Biblical he loveth, and it is only in modern English that it gradually sank to s. In the s of he loves, therefore, we have a demonstrative root, added to the predicative root love, and this s is Originally the same as the Sanskrit ti. This ti again must be traced back to the demonstrative root ta, this or there ; which exists in the Sanskrit demonstrative pronoun tad, the Greek to, the Gothic thata, the English that', and which in Latin we can trace in talis, tantus, tune, tarn, and even in tamen, an old locative in men. We have thus seen that what we call the third person singular of the present is in reality a simple compound of a predicative root with a demonstrative root. It is a compound like any other, only that the second part is not predicative, but simply demonstrative. As in paymaster we predicate pay of master, meaning a person whose office it is to pay, so in dadd-ti^ give-he, the ancient framers of language simply predicated giving of some third person, and this synthetic proposition, give-he, is the same as what we now call the third person singular in the indicative mood, of the present tense, in the active voice.* We have necessarily confined ourselves in our analysis of language to that family of languages to which our own tongue, and those with which we are best acquainted, belong ; but what applies to Sanskrit and the Aryan family applies to the whole realm of tenses, moods, and persons, yields, together with its participles, about 1,300 forms.

human speech. Every language, without a single exception, that has as yet been cast into the crucible of comparative grammar, has been found to contain these two substantial elements, predicative and demonstrative roots. In the Semitic family these two constituent elements are even more palpable than in Sanskrit and Greek. Even before the discovery of Sanskrit, and the rise of comparative philology* Semitic scholars had successfully traced back the whole dictionary of Hebrew and Arabic to a small number of roots, and as every root in these languages consists of three consonants, the Semitic languages have sometimes been called by the name of triliteral.

To a still higher degree the constituent elements are, as it were, on the very surface in the Turanian family of speech. It is one of the characteristic features of that family, that, whatever the number of prefixes and suffixes, the root must always stand out in full relief, and must never be allowed to suffer by its contact with derivative elements.

There is one language, the Chinese, in which no analysis of any kind is required for the discovery of its component parts. It is a language in which no coalescence of roots has taken place ; every word is a root, and every root is a word. It is, in fact, the most primitive stage in which we can imagine human language to have existed. It is language comme il faut ; it is what we should naturally have expected all languages to be.

There are, no doubt, numerous dialects in Asia, Africa, America, and Polynesia, which have not yet been dissected by the knife of the grammarian ; but we may be satisfied at least with this negative evidence, that, as yet, no language which has passed ROOTS. 285 through the ordeal of grammatical analysis has ever disclosed any but these two constituent elements.

The problem, therefore, of the origin of language, which seemed so perplexing and mysterious to the ancient philosophers, assumes a much simpler aspect with us. We have learnt what language is made of; we have found that everything in language, except the roots, is intelligible, and can be accounted for. There is nothing to surprise us in the combination of the predicative and demonstrative roots which led to the building up of all the languages with which we are acquainted, from Chinese to English. It is not only conceivable, as Professor Pott remarks, ' that the formation of the Sanskrit language, as it is handed down to us, may have been preceded by a state of the greatest simplicity and entire absence of inflections, such as is exhibited to the present day by the Chinese and other monosyllabic languages.' It is absolutely impossible that it should have been otherwise. After we have seen that all languages must have started from this Chinese or monosyllabic stage, the only portion of the problem of the origin of language that remains to be solved is this : How can we account for the origin of those predicative and demonstrative roots which form the constituent elements of all human speech, and which have hitherto resisted all attempts at further analysis ? This problem will form the subject of our two next Lectures.
zh: 我們對雅利安語或印歐語族中某些名詞與動詞構成的分析，已經教導我們，儘管這些文法形式乍看之下神秘複雜，但它們實際上是一個非常簡單的過程所造成的結果。乍看之下，提出諸如「為何僅僅添加一個 d 就能將愛的現在式變為愛的過去式」，或「為何法文中的詞尾 ai 加在 aimer 之後，就能傳達愛的未來想法」等問題，似乎幾乎毫無希望。但是，一旦將這些形式置於比較文法的顯微鏡下，這些及其他所有文法形式就會呈現出截然不同且更為可理解的面貌。我們看到了現在所謂的詞尾原本是獨立的詞。在與它們所欲修飾的詞結合之後，它們逐漸被縮減為單純的音節與字母，雖然自身無所指涉，卻通過它們對所附加詞語的意義產生的修飾作用，而展現了它們從前的力量與獨立性。文法詞尾的真實本質最初是由一位哲學家所指出的，儘管他某些推測可能甚為荒唐，但他確實已經領悟到語言的真實生命與發展的許多方面；我指的是霍姆‧圖克（Home Tooke）。以下是他對詞尾所著述的內容：*

4 誠然，我認為自己有充分的理由相信，所有的詞尾同樣可以追溯到各自的源頭；儘管這些詞尾如今在我們眼中顯得極為人工化，但它們最初並非出於深思熟慮的刻意設計之產物，而是獨立存在的詞彙因時間的流逝而腐蝕變形，並與如今被視為詞尾的那些詞彙相融合；然而，這一點較不易為他人所覺察。即便他人曾有所疑慮，他們也將不得不走上一條更加冗長曲折的路途，方能臻于其論證之終端；蓋因那些語言中的腐蝕現象由來已久，較之吾人的語言更為悠遠，亦更為複雜。
然而，霍姆‧圖克雖然正確地看出了應該循著什麼道路去追溯文法詞尾的起源，但他本人卻缺乏手段去完成這段旅程。他的大多數解釋都是站不住腳的；閱讀他的著作《珀利的娛樂》（Diversions of Purley）時，有一種奇異的現象值得注意——一個思路清晰、頭腦敏銳、心智強大的人，其推理遵循著健全而正確的原則，但由於他對事實認識的缺陷，卻得出了與真理直接相悖的結論。
一旦我們看出文法詞尾最初如何可以追溯至獨立的詞彙，我們同時也就學到了語言的構成要素，即那些在我們的研究坩堝中經過完整分析後仍然保留下來的要素。
文法分析來看，根詞（roots）有兩類，即述謂根詞（Roots predicative）與指示根詞（Roots demonstrative）。

我們稱之為「根」或「詞根」的，乃是任何語言或語族中那些無法化約為更簡單或更古老形式的詞素。用幾個例子來說明這一點或許是有益的。但與其取數個梵文、希臘文和拉丁文詞彙，將它們回溯到它們共同的源頭，不如從一個已被發現的詞根開始，追隨它在各語言間的流變會更有啟發性。我採用詞根 AR，我在上一堂課中曾提及它是詞彙 Arya 的源頭，因此當我們考察它的分化時，同時也能了解為什麼農業游牧民族——雅利安種族的祖先——會選擇這個名稱。
這個語根 AR* 的意思是耕耘、開墾土地。由此而來的有拉丁文 ar-are、希臘文 ar-oun、愛爾蘭文 ar、立陶宛文 ar-ti、俄文 ora-ti、哥德文 ar-jan、盎格魯-撒克遜文 er-jan、現代英文的 to ear。莎士比亞曾說過（《理查二世》第三幕第二景）：「耕耘那有望長出莊稼的土地。」我們在《申命記》第二十一章第四節讀到：「一片未曾耕耘也未曾播種的崎嶇山谷。」
由此我們得到了犁或耕作工具的名稱：拉丁文為 ara-trum；希臘文為 aro-tron；波希米亞文為 oradlo；立陶宛文為 arkla-s；康瓦爾文為 aradar；威爾斯文為 arad；（參見 Pott, Etymologische Forschungen, i. 218）；但就我們目前的目的而言，詞根 AR. 已足夠。
如果如人所推測的，康瓦爾語及威爾斯語的詞彙是拉丁文 aratrum 的訛變，它們應當分別出現為 areuder、arawd 的形式。

HOOT AR. 263 古北歐語 ardhr。然而在古北歐語中，ardhr 原意為犁，後來演變為指收入或財富；在早期，犁是最為必要的財產與生計手段。同樣地，拉丁文中表示金錢的詞彙 pecunia 乃衍生自 pecus（家畜）；現今用語 fee（指支付給醫生或律師的費用）在古英語中為 feh，在盎格魯‧撒克遜語中為 feoh，意為家畜與財富；蓋 feoh 與哥德語 faihu 實為同一個詞，與拉丁文 pecus 及現代德語 vieh 相同。
耕田這個動作在拉丁文中稱為 aratio，在希臘文中稱為 arosis；我相信 aroma（香氣、香水之意）也源自同一起源；因為還有什麼比剛耕過的田地的氣味更甜蜜、更芬芳呢？在《創世記》第二十八章第二十七節中，雅各伯說：「我兒子的氣味，如同耶和華所祝福之田地的氣味。」

根詞 ar 更原始的形式似乎是希臘語的 era（土地）、梵文的 ira、古高地德語的 ero、蓋爾語的 ire、irionn。它最初的意義是耕地，後來才泛指土地一般。即使是英語 earth 這個詞——哥德語的 airtha、盎格魯撒克遜語的 eorthe——最初也必然是以耕耘或開墾的土地之義而取用的。衍生詞 ar-mentum（其構成方式如同 ju-mentum）會因為元音的差異而為 arjan。但 airtha 是一個古老得多的形式，來自根詞 ar，而這個根詞本身最初又是 ri 或 ir（Benfey, Kurze Gr. p. 27）。從這個原始根詞 ri 或 ir，我們必須同時推導出梵文的 ira 或 ida，以及哥德語的 airtha。後者相當於梵文的 rita。梵文 ida 的真正意義從未被發現。婆羅門人將其解釋為祈禱，但這並非其原始意義。
自然而然地被應用於任何適合耕地與其他田間勞動的動物身上，無論是牛或馬。

農業是早期社會中的主要勞動，我們必須假設大多數雅利安詞語正是在那個時期形成並被應用於其確定的含義。因此，我們不難理解，一個原本專指這種特殊勞動的詞彙後來如何被用來表達一般的勞動。詞彙及其含義增長中最自然的傾向乃是從特殊到一般：因此 *regere* 和 *gubernare* 原本意為駕駛船隻，後來獲得了統治的一般含義。*To equip*（原本意為配備船隻；法文 *equiper* 和 *esquif* 源自 *schifo*，即船隻）後來開始表示一般的配備。如今在現代德文中，*arbeit* 僅僅意為勞動；*arbeitsam* 意為勤勞的。在哥德語中，*arbaips* 也只用來表達一般的勞動和困苦。但在古北歐語中，*erfidhi* 主要意為犁地，之後才表示一般的勞動；同一詞彙在盎格魯‧撒克遜語中，*earfodh* 或 *earfedhe*，即勞動。當然，我們同樣可以假設，正如 *labourer*（勞動者）從表示一般勞動者的含義後來採取了農業勞動者的特殊含義一樣，*arbeit* 從表示一般的工作，在古北歐語中被應用於犁地的工作。但由於 *erfidhi* 的詞根似乎是 *ar*，我們最初的解釋更為合理。此外，古北歐語中的簡單詞根 *ar* 意為犁地和勞動，古高地德語的 *art* 同樣具有犁地的含義。*arapeit*、現代高地德語 *arbeit*，直接源自哥德語 *arbja*（繼承人）；但承認 *arbja* 與詞根 *AR* 之間的關連。希臘文的 *Aroura* 和拉丁文 *arvum*（田地）應歸於詞根 *ar*（犁地）。由於犁地不僅是最早的勞動方式之一，而且也是最原始的藝術之一，我毫不懷疑，拉丁文 *ars*、*artis* 和我們的詞彙 *art*（藝術），原本意為所有藝術中的藝術，即首先由智慧女神教授給凡人的藝術——耕種土地的藝術。在古高地德語中，*arunti*；在盎格魯‧撒克遜語中，*cerend*，僅僅意為工作；但它們最初也必定意為農業的特殊工作；在英文詞彙 *errand*（差事）和 *errand-boy*（跑腿的男孩）中，同一詞彙至今仍然存在。
然而 ar 不僅僅意指耕地或開墾土地；很早的時期，它就被引申為耕耘大海，或者說划船。因此莎士比亞寫道：—
讓大海為他們服侍；他們以船舟耕耘並傷害它。

同樣地，我們發現梵文從 ar 衍生出名詞 aritra，其意不是指犁，而是指舵。在盎格魯撒克遜語中，我們發現簡單形式 ar，即英文的 oar（槳），彷彿是水的犁頭。希臘人也曾使用詞根 ar 表達划船之義；eretes* 在希臘文中是划船者，而他們的詞 tri-er-es（詞根 arjan，意為犁地）也是如此。他將 arbja 比定為斯拉夫語的 rab（僕人、奴隸），將 arbeit 比定為 rabota（勞役），推測兒子和繼承人是最初天然的奴隸。他甚至推測 rabota 與拉丁文 labor（德文辭典參見 Arbeit 條）之間存在關係。

原本是指有三支槳或三排槳的船隻，*即三列槳戰船。

這種對耕作與划船的比較在古代語言中頻繁出現。英語詞彙 plough、斯拉夫語 ploug，已被辨認為與梵文 plava（船）以及希臘文 ploion（船）相同。由於雅利安人談到船「耕耘」海洋，他們也談到犁「航行」穿過田野；因此同樣的名稱就被應用於兩者。在英語方言中，plough 或 plow 仍被用於「馬車」或「運輸工具」的通俗含義。我們本可繼續追蹤這個根源 ar 的各種分支，但我們在各種語言中已考察的眾多詞彙，已足以說明「述謂根」（predicative root）的含義。在所有這些詞彙中，ar 是根式成分，其餘一切不過是構成成分。根源 ar 被稱為述謂根，因為無論它進入何種組合中，它都表述同一個觀念——無論是關於犁、舵、牛，抑或田野。甚至兩側的槳。
從梵文 plu、希臘文 πλύω：參照英文 fleet 與 float。

其他類似例子：*vvig* 和 *vvviv*（犁鏵），普魯塔克從 *vc*（野豬）推導而來。據稱犁被稱為「豬鼻子」。拉丁文 *porca*（耕地）源自 *porcus*（豬）；德文 *furicha*（犁溝）與 *farah*（野豬）相關。梵文 *vrika*（狼），源自 *vrasch*（撕裂），用來表示犁（《梨俱吠陀》i. 117, 21）。*Godarana*（撕裂大地者）是梵文中犁的另一個詞。哥特語 *hoha*（犁）= 梵文 *koka*（狼）。見格林《德語》及庫恩《印度研究》第一卷第 321 頁。
§ 在黑莫爾谷地，馬車被稱為 plough 或 plow，而 zull（古英語 syl）被用來指稱 aratrum（巴恩斯《多塞特方言》第 369 頁）。
ROOT SPAC. 267 在像 artistic 這樣的詞中，根詞 ar 的述謂力量仍然可以被感知到，雖然當然只能藉由一種強大的望遠鏡才行。自稱為 drya 的婆羅門人在印度，對於這個名字的真實起源及其與農業勞動的關連，並不比現今談論其藝術為神聖靈感的藝術家更加了解——而那位藝術家恰恰沒有意識到他所使用的詞語最初僅適用於像耕地這樣的原始藝術。
我們現在將考察另一個詞族，以便透過何種過程來了解詞彙的根本要素最初是如何被發現的。

讓我們以「respectable」（值得尊敬的）這個詞為例。這是一個拉丁語源而非撒克遜語源的詞彙。在 respectabilis 中，我們容易區分動詞 respectare 與詞尾 bilis。我們隨後分離前綴 re，剩下 spectare，並將 spectare 作為分詞形式追溯到拉丁動詞 spicere 或 specere，意為「看」、「注視」。在 specere 中，我們又可區分可變的詞尾 ere 與不變的殘餘部分 spec，我們稱之為詞根。我們期待在梵文及其他雅利安語言中發現這個詞根；事實上我們確實找到了。在梵文中，較常見的形式是 pa's，意為「看」，不帶 s；但在 spa'sa（間諜）、spashta 與 vi-spashta（清晰的、顯現的）以及吠陀文的 spas（守衛者）中，spa's 也可找到。在日耳曼語族中，我們在古高地日耳曼語的 spehan 中發現這個詞根，意為「看」、「偵察」、「沉思」；而 speha 則是英語的 spy（間諜）。在希臘語中，詞根 spek 已被改變為 skep，它存在於……

在 skeptomai 中，我審視、我檢查；由此衍生 skeptikos，即檢視者或探究者，在神學語言中稱為懷疑者；以及 episkopos，即監督者、主教。現在讓我們考察這個詞根的各種分支。從 respectable 開始，我們發現它最初意指值得尊敬（respect）的人，respect 意為回顧。我們對尋常事物或人物視而不見，但卻轉身再次凝視那些值得我們欽慕、關注、尊敬的對象。這就是 respect 與 respectable 的原初意義，若我們考量到拉丁文 nobilis（高貴的）最初不過傳達了值得被認識的人這一概念，我們就無需為此感到驚訝；因為 nobilis 等同於 gnobilis，正如 nomen 等同於 gnomen，或 natus 等同於 gnatus。
關於「with respect to」（關於、就……而言）一詞，如今幾乎已淪為單純的介詞。蓋若吾人言「With respect to this point I have no more to say」（就這一點而言，我無更多可言），其意與「I have no more to say on this point」（在這一點上，我無更多可言）無異。
再次觀察回溯時，當我們單獨挑選出某一個人，形容詞 respective 和副詞 respectively 的用法幾乎與「特殊的」（special）或「逐一地」（singly）具有相同的含義。
英文的「respite」(暫停、延緩)是對拉丁文 respectus 的諾曼改造，法文為 repit。Repit 最初的意思是回顧、審視全部證據。罪犯獲得若干天的 ad respectum，用以重新檢視案件。後來人們說囚犯獲得了 respit，也就是獲得了重新審理；最後動詞被創造出來，人們說某人被「respited」(獲得緩期)了。

正如 specere（看）加上前綴 re 形成 BOOT SPAC. 269 而意為尊重，同樣地，加上前綴 de（向下）就構成了拉丁文的 despicere，意為向下看，英文的 despise。法文的 depit（古法文 despii）不再表示輕蔑之意，雖然它源自拉丁文的 despectus，而是表示憤怒、煩惱。Se depiter 是指感到煩惱、焦躁。*• En depit de lui* 原意為「對他感到憤怒」，隨後演變為「儘管他」；英文的 spite、in spite of、spiteful 則是 despite、in despite of、despiteful 的簡化形式，與貓的吐口水行為毫無關係。

正如 de 表示「從上向下」，sub 亦表示「從下向上」，將此詞綴加於 specere（看）之上，便得到 suspicere、suspicari，即「向上看」之意，引申為「懷疑」。由此衍生出 suspicion（懷疑）、suspicious（可疑的）；同樣地，法文的 soupçon 亦源於此，即便在「這咖啡裡有一點點菊苣的味道」（There is a soupçon of chicory in this coffee）這樣的措辭中，意思也不過是「一丁點」、「最細微的一絲」菊苣罷了。
既然 circum 意為「周圍」、「四周」，那麼 circumspect 自然就是指謹慎的、小心的。
於「into」之義的 in 與 specere 組合成 inspicere，意為「檢視」；因此衍生出 inspector（檢查官）、inspection（檢查）。
加上前綴詞 ad（朝向），specere 變成 adspicere，意為看著某物。因此產生 adspectus，即事物的外觀、樣貌或形態。

所以 pro（向前）與 specere 結合成 prospicere；由此產生了 prospectus（如同一種眺望）、prospective 等詞彙。con（與‧共同）與 specere 結合成 conspicere（一起看），conspectus；con 與希臘文的 δέρκομαι（我看）相連，也與梵文的 dris 相連。然而在梵文中，更為原始的詞根 dri 或 dar 同樣被保留了下來，並且經常出現，特別是當與前綴詞 a 結合時；如 tad adritya（關於此）。
顯著的。我們之前在 respectable 一詞中已看到，一個新詞彙 spectare 乃由 spicere 的分詞形成。這個詞與前置詞 ex（外、出）結合，給予我們拉丁文 expectare、英文 to expect（期待、向外看）；以及它的衍生詞。
吉祥（auspicious）是另一個詞彙，其次要成分包含了我們的詞根。拉丁文 auspicium 源自 avispicium，原義是觀察某些鳥類，這些鳥被認為對任何公共或私人行為的成功具有吉凶之兆。因此，auspicious 一詞便具有「幸運」的含義。Haru-spex 是賦予那些通過檢視動物內臟來預測未來的人所使用的名稱。
同樣地，從 specere 衍生出 speculum，其意為鏡子或任何觀照自身的工具；從而衍生出 speculari，即英文的 to speculate、speculative 等詞。

但這一個根還有許多分支。因此，拉丁文的 speculum（鏡子）在義大利文中變成了 specchio；同一個字雖然經過迂迴的途徑，卻進入了法文，成為形容詞 espiègle（調皮的、愛捉弄人的）。這個法文字的起源很有意思。德文中存在著一個著名的故事集，大多講述一個半歷史、半神話人物的詭計，這個人物名叫 Eulenspiegel，或稱「貓頭鷹鏡」（Owl-glass）。這些故事被譯成了法文，這位主人公起初被稱為 Ulespiegle，這個名字後來被縮短為 Espiègle，進而成為了所有愛捉弄人的人的通稱。
由於法語不僅從拉丁語借用詞彙，同樣也從日耳曼語族借用，我們在法語中看到拉丁語 specere 的衍生詞與古高地日耳曼語 speȥwan 並行出現，後者略經變形成為 epier（偵察、窺探），以及義大利語的 spiare。日耳曼語中表示「間諜」的詞是 speha，在古法語中作為 espie 出現，在現代法語中則為 espion。

同一根源最多產的分支之一是拉丁文物種（species）。無論我們採取物種的意義是指在持續世代中相似個體的永續延續（居西厄），抑或視其僅作為思想的一個範疇而存在（阿格西），物種原本旨在直譯希臘文的 eidos（形式）相對於 genos（屬、類）。希臘人最初是根據種類和形式對事物進行分類的，雖然這些術語其後經亞里士多德技術性地界定，但其詞源學的意義實則最為恰當。事物可以根據以下兩種方式進行分類：一者因其屬於同一屬或種類，也就是說，因為它們具有相同的起源；這給予我們系譜分類法；或者它們可以因為具有相同的外觀、eidos 或形式而被分類，而無須宣稱它們有共同的起源；這給予我們形態學分類法。然而，正是在亞里士多德的意義上，而非其詞源學意義上，希臘文的 eidos 被譯入拉丁文為 species，意指一屬的細分，一個科族的類別。因此有了法文的 espèce（一種）；英文的 special，意為特定的，相對於普遍的。在「特殊列車」或「特殊信使」這類說法中，根源 spas（看、見）已所剩無幾；然而這種聯繫雖非顯而易見，卻能以完全的確定性予以復原。我們經常聽到「明確說明」（to specify）這一表達。一個人明確說明了他的委屈。這是什麼意思呢？中世紀拉丁文的 specificus 正是希臘文的逐字翻譯

eidopoios。這個詞是指製造或構成某一 eidos（類型）的事物。現在，在分類中，構成物種的是那種特殊性質，它疊加在其他由某個屬的所有成員共同具有的性質之上，從而將一類事物與所有其他類別區別開來。因此，將人類與所有其他動物區別開來的特定性質是理性或語言。所以 specific 一詞遂具有區別性或獨特性的含義，而動詞 to specify 則傳達出逐一列舉或詳細列舉的意思。我最後以法文 epicier 為例——一位值得尊敬的雜貨商，但最初是販售藥物的人。藥劑師必須出售的各種不同藥物，以某種學究氣的說法被稱作 species，不是籠統的藥物，而是特殊的藥物和特別的醫藥。因此藥劑師或藥師在意大利文中仍被稱為 speziale，他的店鋪稱為 spezieria。在法文中，species 依循規律演變為 espece，後來又採取新的形式來表達藥物，即 epices；英文的 spices，德文的 Spezereien。因此才有著名的 pain d'epices（香料麵包）和 epicier（雜貨商）這樣的詞。如果你試圖暫時追溯 spicy 或 well-spiced article 回到簡單的詞根 specere（看），你就會領悟語言那種奇妙的力量：它用少數簡單的元素，創造出眾多名稱，其豐富性幾乎可與無限多樣的大自然本身相提並論。我說「用少數簡單的元素」，是因為 generos 是商品。
可以加入許多衍生詞，例如 specimen、spectator、le spectacle、specialite、spectrum、spectacles、specius、specula 等。
靴子的分類。273 我們所謂的完全謂語根（full predicative roots）數量確實很少，如 ar（耕耘）或 spas（觀看）之類。
根（root）必然是單音節的。*由多於一個音節組成的根總是可以證明為衍生根（derivative roots），即使在單音節根當中，也必須區分原始根、次級根和第三級根。
本原根是那些由（1）一個元音組成的根；例如，i，表示去、行走。
（2）由一個母音和一個子音組成；例如 ad（食）。
（3）一個輔音和一個元音；例如，da，給予。
B. 次要詞根是指由（1）一個輔音、一個元音及一個輔音組成的詞根；例如 tud，意為打擊。
在這些詞根中，要麼是第一個輔音字母，要麼是最後一個輔音字母具有修飾性。
C. 第三級根是由（1）輔音、輔音和母音所構成的；例如 plu，流動之義。
（2）元音、輔音及輔音的組合；例如 ard，意為傷害。
(3) 輔音、輔音、母音及輔音的組合；例如 spa's、to see。
（4）輔音、輔音、元音、輔音、輔音的組合；例如，spand，顫動。
原始根源在《語言科學研究》第 216、311 頁中最為重要。
語言的早期歷史；但由於它們的述謂能力通常過於不明確，無法滿足推進思想的目的，它們很快就被次級和三級的根詞所侵蝕，幾乎被取代。

在次生根中，我們經常可以觀察到「在雅利安語言中，通常是末位的輔音之一易於產生變化。該根保留其一般含義，但此含義因末位輔音的變化而略微改變並得到確定。因此，除了 tud（tudati）外，梵文中還有 tup（topati、tupati、tumpati），意為「打擊」；希臘文 typ-to。我們同樣遇見 tubh（tubhndti、tubhyati、tobhate），意為「打擊」；根據梵文語法學家的說法，還有 tuph（tophati、tuphati、tumphati）。隨後是根 tuj（tunjati、tojati），意為「打擊」、「激發」；另一根 tur（tutorti），同樣被賦予這一含義；又一根 tur（turyate），意為「傷害」。再有更進一步的衍生根 turv（turvati），意為「打擊」、「征服」；有 tuh（tohati），意為「使痛苦」、「使煩惱」；還有 tus（to'tate），梵文語法學家們將「打擊」的含義賦予其上。」

儘管我們可以把所有這些語言詞根稱為根，它們與第一類的關係大致相當於三輔音的閃語族根與更原始的二輔音根之間的關係。* 在第三類中，我們會發現兩個輔音中的一個總是半元音、鼻音或咝音，這些比其他輔音更具變異性；而且我們幾乎總能指出一個輔音是較晚產生的，是為了使其意義更加特殊而加上的二輔音根。因此，除了 spas，我們有詞根 pa's，甚至這個根也被波特（Pott）追溯回更原始的 as。同樣地，vand 只是 vad 根的強化形式，如同 mand 之於 mad，如同 yu-na-j 和 yu-n-j 之於 yuj。詞根 yuj（結合）和 yudh（戰鬥）都指向更古老的 yu 根（混合），而這個簡單的根在梵文中被保留下來。我們完全可以理解，一個具有混合或聚在一起之一般意義的根，應該被用來表達友善的握手和敵對的戰鬥；但我們同樣可以理解，語言在其發展向著清晰和確定性的進展中，應該渴望在這兩個意義之間進行區分，並且樂意利用兩個衍生詞 yuj 和 yudh 來標記這一區分。

梵文語法家已將其語言的整個發展過程簡化為1,706個詞根，*也就是說，他們已經承認這麼多的根式，以便根據他們的語法推導體系，從這些根式推導出梵文中出現的所有名詞、動詞、形容詞、代詞、前置詞、副詞和連詞。然而，根據我們對詞根的解釋，這1,706這個數字將必須大幅縮減，儘管同時也需要添加一些梵文語法家未曾發現的新根式，然而第2、3、5、7、8、9類的詞根數……226個，第1、4、6、10類的詞根……1,480個，其中包括143個第10類的詞根。

表達確定意義的原始音素，進行整部梵文字典所需的詞源學分析，其數量甚至不會達到該數目的三分之一。希伯來文已被約簡為約 500 個語根，*我懷疑梵文是否需要更大的數量。這顯示了原始語言在經濟上的睿智精神，因為為每一個新印象造新語根的可能性幾乎是無限的。縱使我們僅將字母數目定為二十四，二音節與三音節語根加總的可能數字將達 14,400；† 然而中文雖然摒棄複合與衍生，因此比任何其他語言都需要更多的部首，卻僅滿足於約 450 個。用這 450 個音，加上各種聲調與音調所提高到的 1,263，中文已產生了一部包含 4 萬到 5 萬字的字典。‡ 我在《舊約》中計得 5,642 個希伯來文與迦勒底文詞彙。Benloew 估計哥德語的必要部首為 600，現代德語為 250（第 22 頁）。Pott 認為每種語言約有 1,000 個語根。*詞源研究* ii. 第 73 頁。Grimm 已編纂了日耳曼語族中 462 個強動詞的清單。參見《語法》i. 1030。Pott，*詞源研究* ii. 第 75 頁。Dobrowsky，《斯拉夫語言制度》第 2、56 頁，列舉了斯拉夫語族 1,605 個部首。

根據萊布尼茨（Leibniz）的《完美的特性》（*Characteristica Perfecta*, Opp. t. ii. p. 387-388, ed. Dutens）。若字母在字母表中的位置是可變的：23 個字母的拉丁語言有 25,852,016,738,884,976,640,000 種變化；24 個字母的日耳曼語言有 620,448,701,733,239,739,360,000 種。參見波特（Pott）《詞源研究》第二卷第 9 頁；讓‧保羅（Jean Paul）《斐貝爾的人生》第 160 頁。

康熙字典的確切數目達到42,718個。其中約四分之一已成為廢棄用語；其餘部分中約有一半可視為罕見用法，因此實際使用中的詞彙僅約15,000個。 顯然，除了這些述謂性詞根之外，我們還需要另一類根本要素，才能說明語言的完整發展。憑藉400至500個述謂性詞根，語言不會在為所有進入我們認知範圍內的事物造詞時感到困窘。語言是個節儉持家的主婦。試想spas這一個詞根所表達的觀念種類之豐富，你將看到她用500個這樣的詞根，足可組成一部詞典，以滿足人類心智——她丈夫——無論多麼奢侈的需求。若每個詞根衍生出五十個派生詞，我們將得到25,000個詞彙。據一位鄉村教士的可靠證言，他教區中的某些勞動者的詞彙量不超過300個詞。波斯的楔形文字銘文所含詞彙不超過379個，其中131個是專有名詞。古埃及聖賢的詞彙——至少據我們從象形文字銘文所知的部分——約達658個詞。古典風格的義大利歌劇的詞彙亦然。其中許多在現代語言中已不使用，但卻出現在教令文獻和經典著作中。有時在官方文件中也能找到它們，當時人們試圖模仿古風體時尤其如此。其中相當一部分是人名、地名、山名、河名等。欲參選帝國史官之位，有必要掌握9,000個字，這些字已彙編成一部單獨的手冊。——斯坦尼斯拉斯‧儒連
這是布森在其《埃及》第一卷（第453-491頁）所列詞彙表中的字詞數量。然而，這些字詞中有若干雖然聲音相同，但在語源學上必須加以區分；而後來的研究更進一步增加了這個數字。在夏普的《埃及聖書體》（1861年）中的聖書體字群數量達到2,030個。

然而鮮少能夠展現更大的多樣性。* 在英國，一位受過良好教育的人士，曾就讀公立學校與大學，閱讀《聖經》、莎士比亞著作、《泰晤士報》以及繆迪圖書館的所有書籍，在實際交談中很少使用超過約三千至四千個詞彙。那些精確思考與嚴密推理者，避免籠統和模糊的表達方式，等待直到找到確切契合其意旨的詞彙，則會運用更龐大的詞彙庫；而雄辯的演說者可能達到一萬個詞彙的掌握能力。莎士比亞展現了比任何語言中任何作家都可能更豐富的表達多樣性，卻以約一萬五千個詞彙完成了他所有的劇作。彌爾頓的著作建立於八千個詞彙之上；而《希伯來聖經》以五千六百四十二個詞彙說盡其所欲表達者。f 羅伯遜及韋伯斯特辭典中的詞彙數被估為四萬三千五百六十六。然而，據聞托德版的約翰遜辭典包含五萬八千個詞彙，而韋伯斯特的較晚版本已達七萬個詞彙，計入現在時分詞與完成時分詞作為獨立詞彙。弗呂格爾估計其自身辭典中的詞彙數為九萬四千四百六十四，其中六萬五千零八十五為簡單詞，二萬九千三百七十九為複合詞。此乃一八四三年之數據；彼時他表示希冀其下一版本的詞彙數將遠超十萬。這是馬什先生所設定為英文詞彙豐富度（copia vocabulorum）最低限的數字。見《星期六評論》，一八六一年十一月二日。'亞當曼蒂涅斯‧科賴斯在巴黎舊學院辭典中發現二萬九千七百一十二個詞；在約翰遜辭典中三萬六千七百八十四個；在亞美尼亞語詞彙表中五萬個；惟在倫敦版斯特凡努斯寶庫版本中則有十五萬個。' 參見波特，《詞源研究》第二卷，第七十八頁。
瓦羅《拉丁語》第六卷第三十五節：如果這些詞彙的原始詞根有一千個左右，如科斯科尼烏斯所著，那麼從它們的變化形式可以產生約五十萬個詞彙區別，這是因為每個原始詞根通過變化形式大約產生五百種詞形。原始詞根是指諸如 lego（我讀）、scribo（我寫）、sto（我站）、sedeo（我坐）等詞彙，以及其他不是由其他詞彙衍生、而是具有自己詞根的詞彙。

五百個根詞，考慮其多產性與可塑性，對於我們原始祖先的詞典而言已綽綽有餘。然而他們還需要更多東西。倘若他們有一個根詞表達光明與光輝，該根詞可能在太陽、月亮、星辰與天空、白晝、早晨、黎明、春天、歡樂、喜悅、美麗、威嚴、愛、友誼、黃金、財富等名詞中作為述詞。但若要表達「這裡」、「那裡」、「誰」、「什麼」、「這個」、「那個」、「你」、「他」等概念，他們將發現不可能發現任何述詞根詞適用於此目的。誠然，有人曾試圖將這些詞追溯至述詞根詞；但若我們被告知指示根詞 *ta*（此、彼處）可源自述詞根詞 *tan*（延伸），我們在現代語言中發現，指示代詞與語氣詞具有過於原始與獨立之性質，不容如此生硬的解釋。音聲 *ta* 或 *sa*（此、彼處）乃是一種如同任何述詞根詞一般無意識、自然且獨立的表達；且雖然某些此類指示根詞、代詞根詞或局域根詞（所有這些名稱皆曾被應用於它們），可能被追溯至述詞之源，我們必須承認存在一小類獨立的基本詞素，它們在通常意義上並非述詞，而僅是指示、單純表達在某些或多或少確定之局域或時間規定下的存在。
最好至少舉一個代名詞根及其在詞語形成中的影響力的例子。
在某些語言中，特別是在中文裡，一個述語根詞可以自身用作名詞，或 280 指示性根詞。

一個動詞、或一個形容詞或副詞。因此，漢語音節「大」(ta) 無須任何形式變化，就同時表示偉大、偉大性以及「是偉大的」。* 如果「大」(ta) 位於實詞之前，它就具有形容詞的含義。例如「大人」(ta jin) 意為一個偉大的人。如果「大」(ta) 位於實詞之後，它就是謂語，或如我們應該說的，是一個動詞。例如「人大」(jin ta) 或「人大也」(jin ta ye) 就意為「人是偉大的」。† 或者再者，「人惡」(jin ngo)、「禮不惡」(li pu ngo)，就意為「人邪惡」、「禮非邪惡」。由此我們可以看到，根與詞之間完全沒有任何外在的區別，而名詞與動詞的區分，僅憑其在句子中的搭配而已。

在其他語言中，尤其是在雅利安語言中，任何述謂詞根本身都無法形成一個單詞。例如在拉丁文中，有一個詞根 luc，表示「閃耀」。為了得到一個名詞，例如「光」，有必要加上一個代名詞根或指示詞根，這形成了一般的主語，述謂詞根中所包含的意義須對此主語加以述謂。因此，藉由加上代名詞元素 s，我們得到拉丁文名詞 luc-s，「光」，或字面上說，「在此閃耀」。讓我們加上一個人稱代名詞，我們便有了動詞 luc-e-s，「汝閃耀」，汝在閃耀。讓我們加上其他代名詞衍生形式，我們便獲得了形容詞 lucidus、luculentus、lucerna 等等。
然而，這將是完全錯誤的看法。假如兩個詞語像 jin ta 一樣並列，第一個詞可構成第二個詞的述語，第二個詞則用作實詞。因此 jin ta 可能意為「人的偉大」，但在這種情況下，通常更常使用 jin tci ta。
另一例——陳，德；如「人之陳」（jin tchi chen），人的德：陳，有德的；如「陳人」（chen jin），有德的人：陳，贊許；如「陳之」（chen tchi），認為其善：陳，好地；如「陳歌」（chen ko），唱得好。——史坦尼斯拉斯‧朱利安（Stanislas Julien）。

我們若假定所有衍生成分——即從述謂根移除後詞語中所保留的一切——都必須溯源至代詞根，那就必須只看我們現代語言中的一些衍生詞，就足以令人確信，其中許多原本是述謂性的，它們曾與主要述謂根複合，隨後逐漸衰減為純粹的後綴。例如 landscape 中的 scape，以及較現代的 hardship 中的 ship，都來自同一個根源，我們在哥德語中見有 skapa、skop、skopum（創造）；在盎格魯撒克遜語中見有 scape、scäp、scopon。它與德語的衍生詞 schaft（如 Gesellschaft 等）相同。同樣地，wisdom 或 Christendom 中的 dom 也源自我們在 to do 中所見的同一個根源。它與德語的 thum（如 Christenthum）以及盎格魯撒克遜語的 dom（如 cyning-dom、Königthum）相同。有時衍生成分原本究竟是純然示指性的還是述謂性的，可能似乎有所疑難。例如梵文中的比較級詞尾是 tara，希臘文是 teros。乍看之下，此或可視為示指成分，但實則乃根源 tar，其義為超越，此根我們亦見於拉丁文的 trans。這個 trans 在其法文形式 très 中被冠加於形容詞前，以表達更高或超越的程度，而同一個根源適足以在古代雅利安諸語言中形成比較級。此根必如同
我注意到您提供的「英文原文」實際上是古英文詩歌引文（出自埃德蒙·斯賓塞的《牧人月曆》），而非穆勒《宗教學導論》的內容。 此引文過於破損，且文獻背景不明確，難以準確翻譯。請提供： 1. 完整的英文段落 2. 確認其在穆勒著作中的確切位置 3. 前後文脈絡 如此方能提供忠實可靠的繁體中文學術譯文。
（為了你的無首狀態；hood 是表示身份的字尾詞，如 manhood（人的身份）。——T. 沃頓）
我們必須承認在梵文處格（locative）的某個詞尾中有 tra 這樣的形式；例如，從指示詞根 ta 出發，我們可以構成 ta-tra（那裡，原義為「那樣地」）；我們也構成 anyatra（以另一種方式）；這與拉丁文中我們說 ali-ter（源自 aliud）的情況相同；這些複合詞並不比法文 autrement（見第47頁）和英文 otherwise 更令人驚奇。
大多數的格變詞尾和動詞變位詞尾都是指示根，例如第三人稱單數「他愛」(he loves)中的 s，可以證明其原本是第三人稱的指示代詞。它最初並非 s，而是 t。這需要一些解釋。梵文中現在式第三人稱單數的詞尾是 ti。因此 da（給）變成 dadati（他給）；dhā（放置）變成 dadhāti（他放置）。

在希臘語中，這個 *ti* 被改成了 *si*；就像梵文的 *tvam*、拉丁文的 *tu*（你）在希臘語中出現為 *sy* 一樣。因此希臘語的 *didosi* 對應於梵文的 *daddti*；*tithesi* 對應於 *dadhdti*。然而隨著時間推移，希臘語中位於兩個元音之間的每個 *s*（在詞尾中）都被省略了。因此 *genos* 不像拉丁文的 *genus*、*genesis* 或 *generis* 那樣形成屬格 *genesos*，而是 *geneos* = *genous*。與格不是 *genesi*（拉丁文的 *generi*），而是 *gene'i* = *genei*。同樣地，所有規則動詞的第三人稱單數詞尾都是 *ei*。但這個 *ei* 代表的是 *esi*。因此 *typtei* 代表 *typtesi*，而 *typtesi* 代表 *typteti*。
拉丁文省略了末尾的 i，並以 t 代替 ti。因此我們得到 amat、dicit 等形式。

現在讓我提一下之前曾經涉及的一條法則，稱為格林法則（Grimm's Law）。根據這條法則，拉丁文中的每一個清音在哥德語中都用相應的濁擦音來代表。因此，在哥德語中，我們預期應該看到 th 而非 t；我們確實在哥德語 habaty 中發現了這一點，而不是拉丁文的 Tiabet。這個濁擦音同樣出現在盎格魯-撒克遜語中，其中「他愛」為 lufath。它保留在《聖經》英文中的 he loveth，只有在現代英文中它才逐漸弱化為 s。因此，在 he loves 中的 s，我們看到一個指示根（demonstrative root），加在述語根 love 之後，而這個 s 原本與梵文的 ti 相同。這個 ti 又必須追溯到指示根 ta（意為「此」或「彼處」）；它存在於梵文指示代詞 tad、希臘文 to、哥德語 thata、英文 that 中，而在拉丁文中我們可以在 talis、tantus、tune、tarn，甚至在 tamen（一個古老的位置格形式）中追蹤到它。我們因此已經看到，我們所謂的現在式第三人稱單數實際上是述語根與指示根的簡單複合。它是一種複合詞，就像任何其他複合詞一樣，只是第二部分不是述語性的，而純粹是指示性的。正如在 paymaster 中我們述說 pay 的 master，意指其職責為支付者，同樣地在 dadd-ti（給-他）中，古代的語言創造者簡單地述說某第三人稱的給予，而這個綜合命題「give-he」就是我們現在所謂的第三人稱單數直陳式現在式主動語態。我們的語言分析必然將自己侷限於我們自己的語言所屬的語族，以及我們最熟悉的那些語言所屬的語族；但凡適用於梵文和雅利安語族的規律，也適用於整個時態、語氣和人稱的領域，連同其分詞，產生了大約 1,300 種形式。

人類語言。迄今為止，凡經由比較文法的坩堝檢驗過的每一種語言，無一例外，都被發現包含兩個實質性的成分：述謂根（predicative roots）與指示根（demonstrative roots）。在閃語族中，這兩個構成性的成分比在梵語和希臘語中更為明顯。即使在梵語被發現之前，以及比較語言學*興起之前，閃語族的學者已經成功地將希伯來語和阿拉伯語的整個詞典溯源至少量的根詞，而由於這些語言中的每個根詞都由三個輔音組成，閃語族的語言有時因而被稱為三輔音語言（triliteral）。
在圖蘭語族中，構成成分更是以某種方式呈現在最表面的層次上。該語族的一個特徵是，無論前綴和後綴的數量如何，詞根必須始終清晰凸顯，且絕不能因與衍生成分的結合而受損。
有一種語言——漢語——無須進行任何形式的分析，就能發現其組成部分。這是一種根本沒有發生過詞根合併的語言；每個詞都是一個詞根，每個詞根都是一個詞。事實上，這是我們能夠想像人類語言所存在過的最原始階段。它是一種得體的語言（*comme il faut*）；它正是我們自然而然會期待所有語言都應該具備的樣貌。
毫無疑問，亞洲、非洲、美洲及玻里尼西亞地區存在著眾多方言，尚未經過文法家的刀鋒進行剖析；但至少我們可以滿足於這一消極證據，即迄今為止，凡經歷過文法分析考驗的任何語言，都從未揭露出除這兩個組成要素外的其他要素。

因此，語言起源的問題，對於古代哲學家來說曾經顯得如此令人困惑與神祕，在我們的時代卻呈現出簡單得多的面貌。我們已經瞭解語言由什麼構成；我們已經發現語言中除了詞根之外的一切，都是可以理解的，都能夠被解釋的。在述謂根與指示根的組合中沒有什麼足以令我們驚訝，這種組合導致了從中文到英文的所有我們所認識的語言的建立。正如波特教授所指出的，「梵語語言的形成，如我們所傳承的那樣，很可能曾經被先行的一種極端簡樸狀態所介於其前，即完全缺乏屈折變化，正如今日的中文與其他單音節語言所展現的那樣」，這不僅是可以想像的，而且是絕對必然的。在我們已經看到所有語言必然都源自於這種中文或單音節階段之後，語言起源問題中唯一仍有待解決的部分就是這樣的問題：我們如何能夠解釋那些述謂根與指示根的起源，它們形成了全人類言語的組成要素，迄今為止一直頑強地抵抗著所有進一步的分析嘗試？這個問題將構成我們接下來兩次講座的主題。
